transcript: In September 2023, an on-duty sergeant from the Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department was traveling through a construction zone in his unmarked squad car when he spotted a Ford Explorer that closely resembled Indiana State Police vehicles.
The driver, 23-year-old Jonathan Becker, wore a neon green uniform shirt with patches, similar to Indiana law enforcement attire.
Jonathan soon pulled in behind the sergeant and activated his white and amber emergency lights, signaling what appeared to be a traffic stop.
In response, the sergeant pulled over, but Jonathan continued driving, turned off the lights, and was later seen in another construction area with workers dressed in similar attire.
The sergeant asked Jonathan if he was a police officer, but Jonathan denied it and accused the sergeant of speeding in the construction zone.
While they argued, the sergeant called for assistance from uniformed officers.
As they spoke, the sergeant noticed John's tactical belt had a radio mic, a keyring with an Indiana State Police logo, and a black whistle, items commonly used by police officers.

As the sergeant and officers left the area, John was seen taking pictures of their vehicles.
Given the odd and suspicious nature of the situation, the sergeant decided to dig further.
After the incident, the sergeant reached out to other law enforcement agencies to see if they were aware of similar interactions with John.
He received a photograph from June 2022, showing John directing traffic for churchgoers leaving a service.
The photo had been taken by someone who was concerned that the vehicle John was using resembled a fake Indiana State Police vehicle.
The investigation deepened as the sergeant reviewed footage from August 2022, recorded during the funeral procession of an Elwood police officer killed in the line of duty.

Amid the procession of police vehicles with flashing emergency lights, one vehicle stood out, a car operated by John, equipped with red and white emergency lights bearing a license plate that read, Indiana Traffic Operations.
However, the sergeant would soon learn that John made his day-to-day activities well known to the world.
Police discovered numerous photos on his public Facebook profile, showing him dressed in attire similar to law enforcement uniforms.
His bio listed him as the owner of Indiana Traffic Operations LLC since September 2022, though he later removed this information.
Among the posts on his page was a photo of John changing a flat tire, but what drew attention was his utility belt, which clearly displayed a firearm.
Police subsequently reviewed John's TikTok page, Indy Traffic Cops, which contained hundreds of videos showcasing police vehicles, including his own fleet.
In one video, John proudly displayed the interior of a Dodge Charger equipped with emergency lights, and a silver IMPD police badge was visible.
While these badges were never publicly available, they could be acquired by retiring officers.
Following the incident where he pulled over the sergeant, John appeared to change his TikTok username.
Additionally, police found his company's Facebook page, where several posts featured law enforcement related hashtags.
The investigating sergeant believed these posts conveyed a misleading impression that Indiana Traffic Operations was connected to official law enforcement agencies or activities.
The company's website was also reviewed, revealing several professionally taken photos of John.
The sergeant particularly noted the images where John was standing in a public roadway, directing or possibly obstructing traffic, despite there being no apparent reason for his presence there.
In addition, police found John's Instagram page, where his bio stated he was the owner of a page called Law Enforcement.
The account featured over 2,000 posts displaying law enforcement related vehicles.
The sergeant conducted a record search too and confirmed John had never been a police officer with the Indianapolis Metropolitan Police Department or any other agency.

However, he had participated in the IMPD's Explorer program as a high school student in 2016, a detail confirmed by old YouTube videos on his channel, along with other law enforcement related content.

With that, his interactions with real police officers stretch back to 2021, when John was stopped for operating a silver Dodge Charger, outfitted with equipment mimicking that of unmarked police vehicles.
Items like handcuffs and an Ohio State Highway Patrol sticker were discovered.
John was cited and warned about driving a vehicle designed to impersonate law enforcement.
By April 2023, John was stopped again.
This time for moving violations.
A different Dodge Charger, now with state police style decals, carried a firearm on the passenger seat and tools associated with emergency responders.
When officers asked John at the time about the color of the lights on the charger, he replied, get a warrant.
In May 2023, the sergeant reviewed footage from another agency, showing John arriving at an accident scene before first responders.
He blocked a lane of traffic and walked freely among injured parties, police officers, and firefighters.
At one point in the footage, both a police sergeant and a firefighter appeared confused about John's identity and his presence at the scene.
The investigation also revealed John had owned 22 vehicle titles, most of which were police models.
Only four were active at the time of the inquiry.
Based on several of these factors, the investigating sergeant who was initially pulled over by John, concluded that John had falsely portrayed himself as a law enforcement officer.
On October 17th, 2023, the state of Indiana filed a probable cause affidavit.
After a review, probable cause was established and a warrant for John's arrest was issued.
The following day, record showed that John had been taken into custody.
Ultimately, John was charged with impersonation of a public servant, a level six felony offense.
He had also received a class C infraction for operating a motor vehicle with a false plate, and he was released a few days later on a $250 cash bond.
But the story didn't end there.
Just over a year later, John dug himself into deeper shit.

He was arrested once again, this time for felony impersonation of a public servant and unlawfully carrying a handgun in his white Ford F-150, a newer addition to his fleet that he had proudly showcased on his TikTok page shortly before the arrest.
I think this ship has sailed, but why didn't he just apply to be a police officer?
